title: F-GAM2 hardening verdict: lane-level reputation signals
question: Do lane-level reputation signals improve merge outcomes and reduce coordination friction?
key_finding: After 4 waves spanning S186-S405 (219 sessions), reputation tags have never been applied prospectively (at lane open-time). All 3 tagged lanes received tags at close-time in the same session they closed (closure_lag=0 for all 3). This makes the tags documentation artifacts, not coordination signals. The fundamental design assumption — that tags influence downstream behavior — is structurally untestable because the swarm never adopted prospective tagging despite 3 explicit recommendations. The answer to F-GAM2 is: retrospective reputation tags do NOT improve merge outcomes because they carry zero predictive or coordination information. Prospective tagging was never implemented, and after 219 sessions of non-adoption, the mechanism is empirically dead.

evidence.finding: Baseline: 128 total lanes, 0 tagged. reputation_signal_rate_active=0.0. Tagged cohort size=0. No A/B possible.
A/B plan generated: tag 3 lanes (DOMEX-AI/BRN/EVO), keep 3 holdouts. Still 0 tagged at this point. active_tagged_count=0, additional_tagged_needed=3.

Tags rolled out to 3 lanes. reputation_signal_rate_active=0.3. Tagged cohort: mean_updates_per_lane=6.0, conflict_lane_rate=1.0. Untagged: mean_updates_per_lane=1.34, conflict_lane_rate=0.46. Delta: tagged lanes have HIGHER conflict (+0.54) and HIGHER churn (+4.66). Closure lag delta=0.0, handoff lag delta=0.0. No improvement on any metric.

Integrity STRONG (0.94). reputation_signal_rate_active=0.43. Tagged cohort: mean_updates_per_lane=14.0, conflict_lane_rate=1.0. Untagged: mean_updates_per_lane=1.71, conflict_lane_rate=0.49. Delta: tagged still worse on conflict (+0.51) and churn (+12.29). All lag deltas=0.0.

Post-decontamination: reputation_signal_rate_active=0.67, integrity_score=0.97. Tagged cohort: 3 lanes, 1 closed. mean_updates_per_lane=14.33, conflict_lane_rate=1.0. Untagged: mean_updates_per_lane=1.69, conflict_lane_rate=0.54. Still no improvement. Lag deltas remain 0.0.

Post-holdout audit: all 3 originally-tagged lanes (DOMEX-AI/BRN/EVO) closed within same session as tagging. Tags applied at close-time, not open-time. Tags carry no prospective coordination signal. reputation_signal_rate_active <0.5 for new S307 lanes (opened without tags). Structural finding: reputation tags are documentation artifacts, not forward-looking signals.

quantified_summary.merge_rate_tagged: 3/3 (all closed/merged in same session as tagging)

structural_analysis.why_retrospective_tags_cannot_answer_f_g: F-GAM2 asks whether reputation signals IMPROVE merge outcomes. A signal applied after the outcome is determined cannot causally influence that outcome. All 3 tagged lanes were tagged at close-time (same session as closure), so the signal had zero exposure time to influence any downstream behavior (pickup, handoff, conflict resolution). The observed higher conflict rate in tagged lanes (+0.46 to +0.59 delta) is a selection artifact: the 3 tagged lanes were high-activity DOMEX lanes chosen for tagging precisely because they were active and complex, not because tagging made them conflict-prone.
structural_analysis.why_prospective_tagging_never_happened: Wave 3 (S307) explicitly recommended: (1) mandate open-time reliability+evidence_quality tags, (2) add pre-commit guard for untagged new lanes. Neither was implemented across 98 subsequent sessions (S307-S405). This non-adoption is itself evidence: the swarm consistently prioritized other work over F-GAM2 infrastructure, suggesting the expected value of reputation tagging is low relative to competing priorities.
structural_analysis.counterfactual: Even if prospective tagging were implemented now, the baseline merge rate is already 87-97% and closure lag is already 0.0 sessions across the entire population. The ceiling for improvement is extremely low. Reputation tags would need to improve an already-high baseline, making the expected effect size negligible.

recommendation: RESOLVE F-GAM2 as ANSWERED. The answer is: lane-level reputation tags, as implemented (retrospective close-time application), do not improve merge outcomes or reduce coordination friction. They are documentation artifacts. Prospective tagging was never adopted despite explicit recommendations, and the high baseline merge rate (~90%) leaves minimal room for improvement. The frontier has exhausted its exploration budget across 4 waves with a definitive structural finding. No further work is warranted.
